channel.title: The Modal Future
channel.description: Every frontier technology gets two episodes. First, the machine: what physically exists, who builds it, and where the money pools — taught from the ground up, no background assumed. Then, the argument: one committed view of the next decade, with dates attached and the result that would prove it wrong stated in advance. No scenario menus, no hedging — a view you can hold us to.
channel.itunes:author: The Modal Future

item.title: Who Builds the Checkers: Verification Decides the AI Decade
item.description:
A securities filing describes a machine lending money with no human involved — and credits the checking. Insurers reject a hospital AI's billing codes three times less often than its human colleagues'. OpenAI charges ten times more for reliability than for intelligence, on the public price list.
Three documents, one pattern: machines take over paid work exactly where their output can be checked cheaply — and stall, no matter how smart they get, wherever it can't. This episode commits to that thesis: where the boundary runs through the economy today, the ten-month clock it moves on, the September number that will test it, and the result that would prove the whole argument wrong.
Part two of two. Part one toured the machine; this is the argument.

item.title: The Most Expensive Machine Ever Built
item.description:
Ask an AI one question and you switch on the most expensive machine human beings have ever built: an eight-million-dollar rack in Virginia, chips printed with light in Taiwan, a bus-sized machine from one small Dutch town — only about sixty are made each year, for the whole planet — memory stacked like layer cakes in Korea, and electricity ordered three years in advance.
This is the tour of that machine: how intelligence is physically manufactured, who builds each piece, why the bottleneck never disappears — it rotates — and who is actually getting rich.
Part one of two. No background assumed.
